entries.title: Umberto EcoвҖҷs Personal Library Opens to the Public: More Than 32,000 Books, Exactly as He Left Them
entries.summary: Umberto EcoвҖҷs The Name of the Rose sold so well that its English translator William Weaver could add an extension onto his Tuscan villa, which he called the вҖңEco Chamber.вҖқ The Biblioteca Eco, or Eco Library, whose ribbon-cutting earlier this month you can see in the Italian news clip just above, may not be as wittily [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
UmberВӯto EcoвҖҷs The Name of the RoseВ sold so well that its EngВӯlish transВӯlaВӯtor William Weaver could add an extenВӯsion onto his TusВӯcan vilВӯla, which he called the вҖңEco ChamВӯber.вҖқ The BibВӯlioteВӯca Eco, or Eco Library, whose ribВӯbon-cutВӯting earВӯliВӯer this month you can see in the ItalВӯian news clip just above, may not be as witВӯtiВӯly named, but itвҖҷs a much more ambiВӯtious project. It was opened this year,В the tenth anniverВӯsary of the famed philosoВӯpher, semiВӯotiВӯcian, novВӯelВӯist, and critВӯicвҖҷs death, at the UniВӯverВӯsiВӯty of Bologna, where he was a proВӯfesВӯsor from 1971 to 2007. HousВӯing more than 32,000 books from his perВӯsonВӯal library in Milan (preВӯviВӯousВӯly feaВӯtured here on Open CulВӯture, and the subВӯject of its own feaВӯture-length docВӯuВӯmenВӯtary), it stands as an explorable conВӯcrete repВӯreВӯsenВӯtaВӯtion of his capaВӯcious mind.

So capaВӯcious was that mind, in fact, that the Eco Library actuВӯalВӯly comВӯprisВӯes sevВӯerВӯal libraries, chiefly the Rare Book Library and the much-subВӯdiВӯvidВӯed WorkВӯing Library. In them, one encounВӯters secВӯtions вҖңdedВӯiВӯcatВӯed to litВӯerВӯary genВӯres, comics and popВӯuВӯlar culВӯture, works by and about UmberВӯto EcoвҖқ вҖ” includВӯing more than 2,000 ediВӯtions and transВӯlaВӯtions of his own books вҖ” вҖңancient and medieval phiВӯlosВӯoВӯphy, the hisВӯtoВӯry of ideas, semiВӯotics, transВӯlaВӯtion and theВӯoВӯries of lanВӯguage,вҖқ says the UniВӯverВӯsiВӯty of BologВӯnaвҖҷs magВӯaВӯzine UniВӯbo.

There areВ вҖңItalВӯian, French, EngВӯlish-lanВӯguage, GerВӯman, CenВӯtral EuroВӯpean, SlavВӯic, Latin AmerВӯiВӯcan and many othВӯer litВӯerВӯaВӯtures,вҖқВ includВӯing works of вҖңdetecВӯtive ficВӯtion and sciВӯence ficВӯtion, popВӯuВӯlar litВӯerВӯaВӯture and the feuilВӯleton.вҖқ As anyВӯone whoвҖҷs read EcoвҖҷs essay onВ Peanuts might imagВӯine, вҖңhis comВӯic book colВӯlecВӯtion is also of great imporВӯtance.вҖқ

Indeed, as any fan would expect, EcoвҖҷs nameВӯsake library reflects not just the vast sweep of his humanВӯisВӯtic interВӯests and knowlВӯedge, but also hisВ playВӯful sense of eccenВӯtricВӯiВӯty, and even grotesВӯquerie. AccordВӯing to the FonВӯdazione UmberВӯto Eco, most of the volВӯumes it conВӯtains вҖңare marked by a disВӯtincВӯtive ex libВӯris,вҖқ an adapВӯtaВӯtion of a sixВӯteenth-cenВӯtuВӯry woodВӯcut that depicts a donВӯkey on a corВӯnuВӯcopia-topped latrine. вҖңThe aniВӯmal plays a small pipe, setВӯting in motion a merВӯry dance of a dozen cavortВӯing monВӯkeys.вҖқ The books themВӯselves have been painstakВӯingВӯly arranged just as Eco shelved them, in accorВӯdance with the вҖңgood neighВӯborвҖқ prinВӯciВӯple of placВӯing seemВӯingВӯly unreВӯlatВӯed titles in close proxВӯimВӯiВӯty in order to reveal unexВӯpectВӯed intelВӯlecВӯtuВӯal conВӯnecВӯtions between them. The volВӯumes from EcoвҖҷs counВӯtry house in Monte Cerignone will also be incorВӯpoВӯratВӯed into the colВӯlecВӯtion, but as the man himВӯself knew, the best libraries, like the best minds, are nevВӯer truВӯly comВӯplete.

RelatВӯed conВӯtent:

Watch UmberВӯto Eco Walk Through His Immense PriВӯvate Library: It Goes On, and On, and On!

UmberВӯto Eco Explains Why We Make Lists

The Virtue of OwnВӯing Books You HavenвҖҷt Read: Why UmberВӯto Eco Kept an вҖңAntiliВӯbraryвҖқ

DarwinвҖҷs PerВӯsonВӯal Library Goes DigВӯiВӯtal: 330 Books Online

The Largest BookВӯshelf Tour Ever Filmed: Inside a ClassicistвҖҷs 20,000-Volume Library

Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

entries.title: Jimi Hendrix Plays вҖңSgt. PepperвҖҷs Lonely Hearts Club BandвҖқ for The Beatles, Just Three Days After the AlbumвҖҷs Release (1967)
entries.summary: There are many ways to celebrate a new album from a band you admire. You can have a listening party alone. You can have a listening party with friends. You can learn the title track in a couple of days and play it onstage while the band you admire sits in the audience. That last [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
There are many ways to celВӯeВӯbrate a new album from a band you admire. You can have a lisВӯtenВӯing parВӯty alone. You can have a lisВӯtenВӯing parВӯty with friends. You can learn the title track in a couВӯple of days and play it onstage while the band you admire sits in the audiВӯence. That last one might be overkill. Unless youвҖҷre Jimi HenВӯdrix. HenВӯdrix was so excitВӯed after the UK release of Sgt. PepperвҖҷs LoneВӯly Hearts Club Band in 1967 that he opened a set at LondonвҖҷs SavВӯille TheВӯatre with his own, HenВӯdrix-ified renВӯdiВӯtion of the albumвҖҷs McCartВӯney-penned title song. In the audiВӯence: Paul McCartВӯney and George HarВӯriВӯson.

ItвҖҷs a loose, good-natured tribВӯute that, as you might imagВӯine, made quite an impresВӯsion on the BeaВӯtВӯles in attenВӯdance. вҖңItвҖҷs still obviВӯousВӯly a shinВӯing memВӯoВӯry for me,вҖқ McCartВӯney recalled many years latВӯer, вҖңbecause I admired him so much anyВӯway, he was so accomВӯplished.вҖқ

To think that that album had meant so much to him as to actuВӯalВӯly do it by the SunВӯday night, three days after the release. He must have been so into it, because norВӯmalВӯly it might take a day for rehearsal and then you might wonВӯder whether youвҖҷd put it in, but he just opened with it. ItвҖҷs a pretВӯty major comВӯpliВӯment in anyВӯoneвҖҷs book. I put that down as one of the great honВӯours of my career.

McCartВӯney freВӯquentВӯly remВӯiВӯnisces about that night. See him do so in the clip above from an August, 2010 conВӯcert. MacВӯca gushВӯes over HendrixвҖҷs solo, then tells the audiВӯence how JimiвҖ”having thrown his guiВӯtar out of tune durВӯing the solo with his whamВӯmy bar dive-bombingвҖ”asked Eric ClapВӯton to come onstage and retune for him.

ClapВӯton, who McCartВӯney says was actuВӯalВӯly in the audiВӯence, demurred. ItвҖҷs a stoВӯry he conВӯtinВӯues to tellвҖ”in fact.

One linВӯgerВӯing quesВӯtion is whether or not HenВӯdrix knew there were BeaВӯtВӯles present that night. NME and the BBC both say he did not. In a recreВӯation of the moment, above, from the 2013 ficВӯtionВӯalВӯized biopic Jimi: All Is by My Side, HenВӯdrix (played by AndrГ© BenВӯjamin) knows. Not only that, but he decides to open with вҖңSgt. PepperвҖҷsвҖқ right before the gig, with no rehearsal, over the strenВӯuВӯous objecВӯtions of Noel RedВӯding, who thinks the BeaВӯtВӯles might be insultВӯed. ItвҖҷs highВӯly doubtВӯful things went down that way at all. (The scene takes othВӯer licensesвҖ”note the FlyВӯing V instead of the white StraВӯtoВӯcastВӯer HenВӯdrix actuВӯalВӯly played). But it makes for some interВӯestВӯing backВӯstage draВӯma in the film.

In any case, IвҖҷd guess that HendrixвҖ”вҖңthe coolest guy in the world,вҖқ as BenВӯjamin called himвҖ”would have pulled off the covВӯer with panache, whether he knew McCartВӯney was watchВӯing or not. There may be litВӯtle left to say about HenВӯdrixвҖҷs brilВӯliant guiВӯtar theВӯatrics, comВӯpleteВӯly innoВӯvВӯaВӯtive playВӯing style, onstage swagВӯger, and powВӯerВӯful songВӯwritВӯing. But his вҖңSgt. PepperвҖҷsвҖқ covВӯer is an examВӯple of one of his less-disВӯcussed, but highВӯly admirable qualВӯiВӯties: his genВӯuineВӯly aweВӯsome rock and roll colВӯleВӯgialВӯiВӯty.

Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:

Jimi HenВӯdrix Wreaks HavВӯoc on the Lulu Show, Gets Banned From BBC (1969)

Jimi HendrixвҖҷs Final InterВӯview on SepВӯtemВӯber 11, 1970: LisВӯten to the ComВӯplete Audio

Josh Jones is a writer and musiВӯcian based in Durham, NC.

entries.title: Hear the Haunting Sound of an Ancient Celtic War Trumpet: Silent for 2,000 Years, It Now Sounds Again
entries.summary: Norfolk is known for its network of rivers and lakes, its stock of medieval churches, and for being the home county of Alan Partridge. Last year, it quite literally unearthed another mark of distinction in the form of an iron age war trumpet. вҖңThe bronze trumpet or carnyx is only the third ever found in Britain, and [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
NorВӯfolk is known for its netВӯwork of rivers and lakes, its stock of medieval churchВӯes, and for being theВ home counВӯty of Alan ParВӯtridge. Last year, it quite litВӯerВӯalВӯly unearthed anothВӯer mark of disВӯtincВӯtion in the form of an iron age war trumВӯpet.В вҖңThe bronze trumВӯpet or carnyx is only the third ever found in Britain, and the most comВӯplete examВӯple disВӯcovВӯered anyВӯwhere in the world,вҖқ writes theВ GuardianвҖҷs Esther Addley. вҖңFashВӯioned in the shape of a snarling wild aniВӯmal, the object would have been mountВӯed on a long mouthВӯpiece high above the heads of warВӯriors, allowВӯing it to be soundВӯed to intimВӯiВӯdate the eneВӯmy in batВӯtle.вҖқ One could hardВӯly hope for a more powВӯerВӯful symВӯbol of Celtic fearВӯsomeВӯness.

Yet вҖңthere are many conВӯtraВӯdicВӯtionsвҖқ in the longer stoВӯry of the carnyx, says enviВӯronВӯmenВӯtal sciВӯenВӯtist Paul Whitewick in his new video, вҖңfrom where itвҖҷs been found to its intendВӯed use, its depicВӯtion on art and coins: none of those seem to make sense.вҖқ The most famous head of a carnyx was uncovВӯered in a ScotВӯtish peat bog; anothВӯer came out of the RivВӯer WithВӯam in LinВӯcolnshire.

In addiВӯtion to those recovВӯerВӯies from non-batВӯtleВӯfield setВӯtings, one detailed ironВӯwork panВӯel comВӯmonВӯly refВӯerВӯenced in carnyx reconВӯstrucВӯtion presents its use in more of a proВӯcesВӯsion. It also became a symВӯbol on Roman-era coinage, and with an apparВӯentВӯly wide range of meanВӯing. PerВӯhaps the carnyx was a war horn; perВӯhaps it was much more.

WherВӯevВӯer the ancients typВӯiВӯcalВӯly broke them out, carnyces arenвҖҷt for disВӯcussing, but for hearВӯing. None was soundВӯed for two milВӯlenВӯnia, until the reconВӯstrucВӯtion of the one found in the peat bog, known as the DeskВӯford Carnyx. The first musiВӯcian to give it a blow, in 1993, was John KenВӯny, whoвҖҷs been known as the worldвҖҷs foreВӯmost carnyx playВӯer ever since. He does so in the video just above from NationВӯal MuseВӯums ScotВӯland, which offers not just the instruВӯmenВӯtвҖҷs hauntВӯing sound, but a close-up view of its wagВӯging tongue. KenВӯny, too, believes that carnyces have uses beyond war, and even that вҖңthey are more impresВӯsive when they are played less loudВӯly.вҖқ Still, just imagВӯine how intimВӯiВӯdatВӯed an eneВӯmy army would feel upon hearВӯing a whole line of them soundВӯed at once вҖ” to say nothВӯing of seeВӯing all those tongues.

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:

Bronze Age Britons Turned Bones of Dead RelВӯaВӯtives into MusiВӯcal InstruВӯments & OrnaВӯments

Hear a PreВӯhisВӯtoric Conch Shell MusiВӯcal InstruВӯment Played for the First Time in 18,000 Years

Hear the WorldвҖҷs OldВӯest InstruВӯment, the вҖңNeanВӯderthal Flute,вҖқ DatВӯing Back Over 43,000 Years

Hear the TripВӯpy MysВӯtiВӯcal Sounds of Giant Gongs

Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

entries.title: Getting Dressed During World War I: A Fascinating Look at How Soldiers, Nursers & Others Dressed During the Great War
entries.summary: Not to diminish the nightmare of mortars and shrapnel, but as evidenced by CrowвҖҷs Eye ProductionsвҖҷ period-accurate dressing video above, one of the greatest horrors of WWI was wet wool. Decades before the invention of Gore-Tex, Polar Fleece, and other high-performance, all-weather gear, British soldiers relied on their woolies from head to toe. An army [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
Not to diminВӯish the nightВӯmare of morВӯtars and shrapВӯnel, but as eviВӯdenced by CrowвҖҷs Eye ProВӯducВӯtionsвҖҷ periВӯod-accuВӯrate dressВӯing video above, one of the greatВӯest horВӯrors of WWI was wet wool.

Decades before the invenВӯtion of Gore-Tex, Polar Fleece, and othВӯer high-perВӯforВӯmance, all-weathВӯer gear, British solВӯdiers relied on their woolies from head to toe.

An army of female knitВӯters sent gloves, scarves, balВӯaВӯclavas, and othВӯer such вҖңcomВӯfortsвҖқ to the front, in addiВӯtion to seamВӯless socks designed to last their boys three whole marchВӯing days inside their ankle-high leather boots.

Alas, no amount of waxВӯing and oilВӯing could keep the trenchВӯesвҖҷ freezВӯing cold pudВӯdles from seepВӯing through those boots.

NothingвҖҷs worse than the scent of three layВӯers of wet wool when youвҖҷre catchВӯing your death in sodВӯden putВӯtees.

The regВӯiВӯments whose uniВӯform botВӯtoms conВӯsistВӯed of kilts had it parВӯticВӯuВӯlarВӯly rough, as the wet mateВӯrВӯiВӯal would freeze, cutВӯting across the wearВӯersвҖҷ legs like knives.

PreВӯventВӯed from joinВӯing the comВӯbat on the frontВӯlines, British women helped out where they could, achievВӯing a more comВӯfortВӯable levВӯel of dress than theyвҖҷd known before the war.

TorВӯso-smashВӯing corsets were scrapped to preВӯserve steel for the war effort, though decoВӯrum decreed that British Red Cross SociВӯety VolВӯunВӯtary Aid DetachВӯment nursВӯes, such as DownВӯton AbbeyвҖҷs ficВӯtionВӯal Lady Sybil CrawВӯley, mainВӯtain a tidy figВӯure with lighter, front-fasВӯtenВӯing corsets from hips to just below the bust.

Many of the upper-class women swelling the volВӯunВӯteer nursВӯing ranks were unacВӯcusВӯtomed to dressВӯing in such utilВӯiВӯtarВӯiВӯan fashionвҖ”cotton dressВӯes, black flat rubВӯber-soled shoes, aprons and sleeve proВӯtecВӯtors.

Their figВӯures found comВӯparВӯaВӯtive libВӯerВӯaВӯtion, while their vanВӯiВӯty found humВӯbler outВӯlets in dustВӯing powВӯder and the flatВӯterВӯing army-style proВӯfesВӯsionВӯal nursВӯing veils they preВӯferred to The HandmaidвҖҷs Tale-ish SisВӯter Dora caps.

The greater physВӯiВӯcal freeВӯdom of the nursВӯesвҖҷ uniВӯforms extendВӯed to ordiВӯnary young women as well. Their underwearвҖ”a midriff-barВӯing chemise, knickВӯers and petticoatвҖ”allowed for easВӯiВӯer moveВӯment, as shortВӯer skirts led to glamВӯorous stockВӯings andвҖ”gasp!вҖ”shaved legs!

Trendy cardiВӯgans, jumpers and waistВӯcoats werenвҖҷt just cute, they helped make up for the lost warmth of those oh-so-restricВӯtive corsets.

View more of CrowвҖҷs Eye ProВӯducВӯtionsвҖҷ short films on the hisВӯtoВӯry of dress here.

KnitВӯters, you can find over 70 patВӯterns for WW1 comВӯforts and necesВӯsiВӯties in the book CenВӯteВӯnary StitchВӯes: Telling the StoВӯry of One WW1 FamВӯiВӯly Through VinВӯtage KnitВӯting and CroВӯchet.

Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:

The DressВӯer: The ConВӯtrapВӯtion That Makes GetВӯting Dressed an AdvenВӯture

How to Make and Wear Medieval Armor: An In-Depth Primer

Ayun HalВӯlВӯiВӯday is an author, illusВӯtraВӯtor, and theВӯater makВӯer in NYC.

entries.title: The WorldвҖҷs Greatest Song That Simply ShouldnвҖҷt Exist: The Making of The BeatlesвҖҷ вҖңA Day in the LifeвҖқ
entries.summary: Were we to take bets on the single most widely heard alarm clock, the one included on the BeatlesвҖҷ вҖңA Day in the LifeвҖқ would surely be a popular choice. It comes, youвҖҷll recall, at the beginning of the songвҖҷs middle section, when Paul takes over from John and sings about waking up, combing his [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
Were we to take bets on the sinВӯgle most wideВӯly heard alarm clock, the one includВӯed on the BeaВӯtВӯlesвҖҷ вҖңA Day in the LifeвҖқ would sureВӯly be a popВӯuВӯlar choice. It comes, youвҖҷll recall, at the beginВӯning of the songвҖҷs midВӯdle secВӯtion, when Paul takes over from John and sings about wakВӯing up, combВӯing his hair, catchВӯing a bus, and havВӯing a smoke. GivВӯen the lyricsвҖҷ subВӯject matВӯter, its incluВӯsion seems natВӯurВӯal, and even like one of the most straightВӯforВӯwardВӯly explicВӯaВӯble choicВӯes in the entire recordВӯing. In fact, the alarm clock was nevВӯer meant to stay in the mix: the bandвҖҷs assisВӯtant Mal Evans set it off as a gag after doing a placeВӯholdВӯer count of 24 meaВӯsures in the midВӯdle of the sesВӯsions, but it just hapВӯpened to work perВӯfectВӯly with the form the song evenВӯtuВӯalВӯly took.

ThatвҖҷs just one of the forВӯtuВӯitous acciВӯdents music YouTuВӯber David HartВӯley recounts in the video above, on the comВӯplete makВӯing of вҖңA Day in the Life.вҖқ As most BeaВӯtВӯles fans know, John realВӯly did take his iniВӯtial inspiВӯraВӯtion from the newsВӯpaВӯper, and in parВӯticВӯuВӯlar a couВӯple of artiВӯcles about a GuinВӯness heirвҖҷs fatal car crash and 4,000 holes in BlackВӯburn, LanВӯcashire.

But after he wrote his versВӯes, he found himВӯself at a loss as to how to develВӯop the song from there. Paul offered the bit heвҖҷd already writВӯten about falling out of bed and dragВӯging a comb across his head. It wasВӯnвҖҷt long before the BeaВӯtВӯles began recordВӯing вҖ” and doing so for the first time, HartВӯley notes, before havВӯing finВӯished writВӯing the song. They had disВӯparate pieces of music to join togethВӯer, but no idea, yet, of how theyвҖҷd do it.

Hence those 24 empВӯty bars. PlayВӯing a series of earВӯly takes, HartВӯley shows how they got to be filled in, drawВӯing heavВӯiВӯly on Here, There, and EveryВӯwhere: My Life RecordВӯing the Music of the BeaВӯtВӯles, the memВӯoir of engiВӯneer Geoff EmerВӯick. JohnвҖҷs idea of вҖңa sound that began realВӯly small, and then gradВӯuВӯalВӯly expandВӯed into someВӯthing hugeвҖқ inspired PaulвҖҷs sugВӯgesВӯtion of a symВӯphoВӯny orchesВӯtra, in response to which proВӯducВӯer George MarВӯtin expressed conВӯcerns about the cost of hirВӯing one. Ringo, in turn, jokВӯingВӯly proВӯposed hirВӯing half the orchesВӯtra and havВӯing them play the part twice, which turned out to be a pragВӯmatВӯic soluВӯtion. As for how to end a song like this, the use of вҖңmaybe the most famous sinВӯgle chord in recordВӯed hisВӯtoВӯryвҖқ вҖ” folВӯlowed by вҖңa high-freВӯquenВӯcy tone only dogs could hear and a short loop of nonВӯsense soundвҖқ вҖ”В came as the result of a process we can only call BeatВӯlesque.

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:

How вҖңStrawВӯberВӯry Fields ForВӯevВӯerвҖқ ConВӯtains вҖңthe CraВӯziВӯest EditвҖқ in BeaВӯtВӯles HisВӯtoВӯry

entries.title: Where Did the MonkвҖҷs Haircut Come From? A Look at the Rich and Contentious History of the Tonsure
entries.summary: One might assume from a modern viewpoint that the hairstyles worn by monks arose to deal with male pattern baldness anxiety. As in the school uniform approach, you canвҖҷt single out one personвҖҷs baldness when everyone is bald. But this, again, would be a modern view, full of the vanity the tonsuredвҖ”those with religiously shaven [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
One might assume from a modВӯern viewВӯpoint that the hairВӯstyles worn by monks arose to deal with male patВӯtern baldВӯness anxВӯiВӯety. As in the school uniВӯform approach, you canвҖҷt sinВӯgle out one personвҖҷs baldВӯness when everyВӯone is bald. But this, again, would be a modВӯern view, full of the vanВӯiВӯty the tonsuredвҖ”those with reliВӯgiousВӯly shaven headsвҖ”ostensibly vowed to renounce. AccordВӯing to the Catholic EncyВӯcloВӯpeВӯdia, the tonВӯsure (from the Latin verb for вҖңto shearвҖқ) began as a вҖңbadge of slavВӯeryвҖқ among Greeks and Romans. It was adoptВӯed вҖңon this very accountвҖқ by earВӯly monasВӯtic orders, to mark the total surВӯrenВӯder of the will.

Would it surВӯprise you, then, to learn that there were tonВӯsure wars? ProbВӯaВӯbly not if you know anyВӯthing about church hisВӯtoВӯry. Every artiВӯcle of clothВӯing and of faith has sparked some major conВӯtroВӯverВӯsy at one time or anothВӯer. So too with the tonВӯsure, of whichвҖ”we learn in the Vox video aboveвҖ”there were three styles. The first, the coroВӯnal (or Roman or Petrine) tonВӯsure, is the one we see in countВӯless Medieval and RenaisВӯsance paintВӯings: a bald pate at the crown surВӯroundВӯed by a fringe of hair, posВӯsiВӯbly meant to evoke the crown of thorns. Next is the Pauline, a fulВӯly shaved head, seen much less in WestВӯern art since it was вҖңused more comВӯmonВӯly in EastВӯern OrthoВӯdoxy.вҖқ

The third style of tonВӯsure caused all the trouВӯble. Or rather, it was this style that served as a visВӯiВӯble sign of reliВӯgious difВӯferВӯences between the Roman Catholic Church and the churchВӯes in Britain and IreВӯland. вҖңCeltic CatholiВӯcism was вҖҳout of syncвҖҷ with the Roman Catholic Church,вҖқ notes Vox. вҖңRoman Catholics would use the difВӯferВӯences between them to porВӯtray Celtic CatholiВӯcism as pagan, or even as an offВӯshoot, celВӯeВӯbratВӯing the powВӯer-hunВӯgry magiВӯcian, Simon Magus.вҖқ The Celtic tonВӯsure fell under a cloud, but how exactВӯly did it difВӯfer from the othВӯers? Since it disВӯapВӯpeared in the earВӯly MidВӯdle Ages and few images seem to have surВӯvived, no one seems sure.

Daniel McCarthy, felВӯlow emerВӯiВӯtus at TrinВӯiВӯty ColВӯlege, Dublin set out to solve the mysВӯtery. He specВӯuВӯlates that the Celtic tonВӯsure, as youвҖҷll see on a comВӯputВӯer-simВӯuВӯlatВӯed monkвҖҷs head, was a triВӯanВӯguВӯlar shape, with the apex at the front. When the Roman Catholics took over IreВӯland, all of the vestВӯments, dates, and hairВӯcuts were slowВӯly brought into line with the domВӯiВӯnant view. The pracВӯtice of tonВӯsure offiВӯcialВӯly endВӯed in 1972, and fell out of favor in EngВӯlish-speakВӯing counВӯtries cenВӯturies earВӯliВӯer, accordВӯing to the Catholic EncyВӯcloВӯpeВӯdia. But in any case, McCarthy sees the tonВӯsure not as a spurnВӯing of fashВӯion, but as a cult-like devoВӯtion to style. In that sense, we can see peoВӯple who adopt simВӯiВӯlar hairВӯcuts around the world as still visuВӯalВӯly sigВӯnalВӯing their memВӯberВӯship in some kind of order, reliВӯgious or othВӯerВӯwise.

Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:

How a BalВӯtiВӯmore HairВӯdressВӯer Became a World-Renowned вҖңHair ArchaeВӯolВӯoВӯgistвҖқ of Ancient Rome

AniВӯmatВӯed: Stephen Fry & Ann WidВӯdeВӯcombe Debate the Catholic Church

50 Years of ChangВӯing David Bowie Hair Styles in One AniВӯmatВӯed GIF

Josh Jones is a writer and musiВӯcian based in Durham, NC.

entries.title: The Radical, Unclassifiable Art of William Blake
entries.summary: William Blake is a household name, or not far from it, but things get more complicated when it comes to professional description. He was a poet, a painter, and a printmaker, at least insofar as he wrote poetry, painted paintings, and made prints. But we canвҖҷt hope to attain even a basic understanding of his [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
William Blake is a houseВӯhold name, or not far from it, but things get more comВӯpliВӯcatВӯed when it comes to proВӯfesВӯsionВӯal descripВӯtion. He was a poet, a painter, and a printВӯmakВӯer, at least insoВӯfar as he wrote poetВӯry, paintВӯed paintВӯings, and made prints. But we canвҖҷt hope to attain even a basic underВӯstandВӯing of his legaВӯcy if we regard him as one man who hapВӯpened to have the enerВӯgy to do three difВӯferВӯent things. In fact, the ostenВӯsiВӯbly sepВӯaВӯrate artisВӯtic purВӯsuits in which he engaged were but three aspects of a uniВӯfied act of creВӯation, resultВӯing in the likes of Songs of InnoВӯcence and of ExpeВӯriВӯenceВ and his illuВӯmiВӯnatВӯed вҖңprophetВӯic booksвҖқ: unclasВӯsiВӯfiВӯable works by вҖңthe patron saint of unclasВӯsiВӯfiВӯable artists.вҖқ

So Evan Puschak, betВӯter known as the NerdВӯwriter, labels him in his new video above. Blake made books, вҖңwhich he designed, wrote, etched, colВӯored, and printВӯed himВӯself, using a techВӯnique that he inventВӯed.вҖқ They вҖңmix and synВӯtheВӯsize catВӯeВӯgories, and as a result, the artВӯwork of the late sevВӯenВӯteen- and earВӯly eighВӯteen-hunВӯdreds didВӯnвҖҷt realВӯly know what to make of them.вҖқ

It didВӯnвҖҷt help that the RoyВӯal AcadВӯeВӯmy of Arts, foundВӯed when Blake was in adoВӯlesВӯcence, had laid down its own strict aesВӯthetВӯic, generВӯic, and forВӯmal stanВӯdards. OffiВӯcialВӯly an engraver and accordВӯed the lowВӯly staВӯtus thereВӯof, Blake devotВӯed his labors to realВӯizВӯing his elabВӯoВӯrateВӯly idioВӯsynВӯcratВӯic visions using images and words togethВӯer in ways that no artist had done before.

This choice вҖңto work as makВӯer of words, makВӯer of images, and crossВӯbreedВӯer of both, amountВӯed to a deciВӯsion to live in incomВӯmenВӯsuВӯrable neighВӯborВӯhoods of meanВӯing,вҖқВ writesВ The CamВӯbridge ComВӯpanВӯion to William Blake author MorВӯris Eaves as quotВӯed by Puschak. Blake swam against the curВӯrent вҖңof modВӯern human underВӯstandВӯing, whose bedrock is the prinВӯciВӯple of speВӯcialВӯizaВӯtion.вҖқ Today, nearВӯly two cenВӯturies after his death, that prinВӯciВӯple still obtains, and in some ways more rigidВӯly than ever. But his work remains, from the SongsВ and the prophetВӯic books to his illusВӯtraВӯtions of clasВӯsics like theВ Divine ComВӯeВӯdy,В ParВӯadise Lost, and even the BibВӯliВӯcal Book of Job, which he read as вҖңthe stoВӯry of a man who believes, misВӯtakВӯenВӯly, that salВӯvaВӯtion requires a slavВӯish obeВӯdiВӯence to words writВӯten in a book.вҖқ With hisВ rules, diviВӯsions, and catВӯeВӯgories, man has cutВ realВӯiВӯty apart;В through his art, Blake sought to make it whole again.

RelatВӯed conВӯtent:

The RadВӯiВӯcal ArtisВӯtic & PhiloВӯsophВӯiВӯcal World of William Blake: A Short IntroВӯducВӯtion

William BlakeвҖҷs HalВӯluВӯciВӯnaВӯtoВӯry IllusВӯtraВӯtions of John MiltonвҖҷs ParВӯadise Lost

The OthВӯerВӯworldВӯly Art of William Blake: An IntroВӯducВӯtion to the VisionВӯary Poet and Painter

William Blake: The RemarkВӯable PrintВӯing Process of the EngВӯlish Poet, Artist & VisionВӯary

entries.title: A Walk Through HomerвҖҷs Odyssey: A Guide to the Epic Before Seeing Christopher NolanвҖҷs Film
entries.summary: YouвҖҷre gearing up to see Christopher NolanвҖҷs Odyssey, but you havenвҖҷt read the Homeric work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels like youвҖҷve never read it at all. No worries. Above, Tom Holland and Dominic Sandbrook, the hosts of The Rest Is History podcast, take you through the major plot lines [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
YouвҖҷre gearВӯing up to see ChristoВӯpher NolanвҖҷs Odyssey, but you havenвҖҷt read the HomeВӯrВӯic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels like youвҖҷve nevВӯer read it at all. No worВӯries. Above, Tom HolВӯland and Dominic SandВӯbrook, the hosts of The Rest Is HisВӯtoВӯry podВӯcast, take you through the major plot lines of the Odyssey, unpackВӯing the litВӯerВӯary and hisВӯtorВӯiВӯcal meanВӯing of the epicвҖҷs difВӯferВӯent tales. ItвҖҷs a good primerвҖ”just what you need to get ready for one of the bigВӯger cinВӯeВӯma releasВӯes this year. Enjoy!

If you would like to supВӯport the misВӯsion of Open CulВӯture, conВӯsidВӯer makВӯingВ a donaВӯtion to our site. ItвҖҷs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conВӯtriВӯbuВӯtions will help us conВӯtinВӯue proВӯvidВӯing the best free culВӯturВӯal and eduВӯcaВӯtionВӯal mateВӯriВӯals to learnВӯers everyВӯwhere. You can conВӯtribute through PayВӯPal, PatreВӯon, and VenВӯmo (@openculture). Thanks!

entries.title: The Origins of the Monsters in HomerвҖҷs Odyssey: The Cyclops, Sirens, Scylla & More
entries.summary: Despite having been composed about two and a half millennia before the invention of cinema, HomerвҖҷs Odyssey has offered tempting material to generation after generation of filmmakers. Part of the appeal is, of course, the workвҖҷs age, which obviates the need for potentially frustrating rights negotiations. But what really captures a directorвҖҷs imagination about retelling the story [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
Despite havВӯing been comВӯposed about two and a half milВӯlenВӯnia before the invenВӯtion of cinВӯeВӯma,В HomeВӯrвҖҷsВ Odyssey has offered temptВӯing mateВӯrВӯiВӯal to genВӯerВӯaВӯtion after genВӯerВӯaВӯtion of filmВӯmakВӯers. Part of the appeal is, of course, the workвҖҷs age, which obviВӯates the need for potenВӯtialВӯly frusВӯtratВӯing rights negoВӯtiВӯaВӯtions. But what realВӯly capВӯtures a direcВӯtorвҖҷs imagВӯiВӯnaВӯtion about retelling the stoВӯry of OdysseusвҖҷ long jourВӯney back to IthaВӯca must have a great deal to do with the host of monВӯsters he encounВӯters along the way. The giant canВӯniВӯbal LaestryВӯgoВӯniВӯans; the sirens, whose call forces Odysseus to lash himВӯself to the mast of his ship; ScylВӯla andВ CharybВӯdis, guardians of the Strait of MessiВӯna; and perВӯhaps most memВӯoВӯrably of all, the towВӯerВӯingВ cyclops PolypheВӯmus.

Many or most of these fearВӯsome charВӯacВӯters are familВӯiar to us even if weвҖҷve nevВӯer read theВ Odyssey, or indeed seen any of its adapВӯtaВӯtions. In everyВӯday speech, we invoke the sirensвҖҷ call when describВӯing an irreВӯsistible tempВӯtaВӯtion, orВ ScylВӯla and CharybВӯdis when describВӯing any set of equal and oppoВӯsite pitВӯfalls. And it would be a rare man, woman, or even sufВӯfiВӯcientВӯly eduВӯcatВӯed child who canвҖҷt idenВӯtiВӯfy the definВӯing feaВӯture of a cyclops.

But long before all of these could enter the modВӯern lexВӯiВӯcon, they had to be inventВӯed in antiqВӯuiВӯty. In the new HochelaВӯga video above, host TomВӯmie Trelawny invesВӯtiВӯgates their oriВӯgins, going over theВӯoВӯries that sugВӯgest that some or all of these monВӯsters had already made fairВӯly long culВӯturВӯal jourВӯneys of their own before Homer put them in OdysseusвҖҷ path.

The myth of the cyclops could have been inspired by eleВӯphant skulls with large cenВӯtral nasal cavВӯiВӯties, or perВӯhaps by a brutish inverВӯsion of eyes as a sigВӯnal of intelВӯliВӯgence. It could have been a series of colosВӯsal Bronze Age stone statВӯues on the island of SarВӯdinia that conВӯstiВӯtutВӯed the basis for the LaestryВӯgoВӯniВӯans. As for the sirens, which we imagВӯine as beauВӯtiВӯful women, the pre-ChrisВӯtВӯian ancient Greeks enviВӯsioned them as strange winged creaВӯtures makВӯing promisВӯes of knowlВӯedge. ScylВӯla and CharybВӯdis, repВӯreВӯsenВӯtaВӯtions of the destrucВӯtive forces of nature, were a way of reifyВӯing the Strait of MessiВӯnaвҖҷs inherВӯent perВӯils. WhatВӯevВӯer their oriВӯgins, all these chalВӯlengers to OdysseusвҖҷ homeВӯcomВӯing still fire up the imagВӯiВӯnaВӯtions of filmВӯmakВӯers, espeВӯcialВӯly filmВӯmakВӯers inclined to high-tech specВӯtaВӯcle: ChristoВӯpher Nolan, for instance, the theВӯatriВӯcal release of whose Odyssey begins tomorВӯrow. We all know that the hero gets home in the end, but weвҖҷll buy tickВӯets for the monВӯsters.

RelatВӯed conВӯtent:

Watch the First SpecВӯtacВӯuВӯlar Film AdapВӯtaВӯtion of the Odyssey (1911)

Hear What HomerвҖҷs Odyssey SoundВӯed Like When Sung in the OrigВӯiВӯnal Ancient Greek

MemenВӯto Mori: How SmilВӯing SkeleВӯtons Have RemindВӯed Us to Live FulВӯly Since Ancient Times

entries.title: Hear Hours of Lectures by Michel Foucault: Recorded in English & French Between 1961 and 1983
entries.summary: Tucked in the afterward of the second, 1982 edition of Hubert Dreyfus and Paul RabinowвҖҷs Michel Foucault: Beyond Structuralism and Hermeneutics, we find an important, but little-known essay by Foucault himself titled вҖңThe Subject and Power.вҖқ Here, the French theorist offers what he construes as a summary of his lifeвҖҷs work: spanning 1961вҖҷs Madness and [вҖҰ]
entries.content:
Tucked in the afterВӯward of the secВӯond, 1982 ediВӯtion of Hubert DreyВӯfus and Paul RabinowвҖҷs Michel FouВӯcault: Beyond StrucВӯturalВӯism and HermeneuВӯtics, we find an imporВӯtant, but litВӯtle-known essay by FouВӯcault himВӯself titled вҖңThe SubВӯject and PowВӯer.вҖқ Here, the French theВӯoВӯrist offers what he conВӯstrues as a sumВӯmaВӯry of his lifeвҖҷs work: spanВӯning 1961вҖҷs MadВӯness and CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtion up to his three-volВӯume, unfinВӯished HisВӯtoВӯry of SexВӯuВӯalВӯiВӯty, still in progress at the time of his death in 1984. He begins by telling us that he has not been, priВӯmarВӯiВӯly, conВӯcerned with powВӯer, despite the wordвҖҷs appearВӯance in his essayвҖҷs title, its arguВӯments, and in nearВӯly everyВӯthing else he has writВӯten. Instead, he has sought to disВӯcovВӯer the вҖңmodes of objecВӯtiВӯfiВӯcaВӯtion which transВӯform human beings into subВӯjects.вҖқ

This disВӯtincВӯtion may seem abstruse, a needВӯlessВӯly wordy matВӯter of semanВӯtics. It is not so for FouВӯcault. In this key critВӯiВӯcal difВӯferВӯence lies the origВӯiВӯnalВӯiВӯty of his project, in all its varВӯiВӯous stages of develВӯopВӯment. вҖңPowВӯer,вҖқ as an abstracВӯtion, an objecВӯtive relaВӯtion of domВӯiВӯnance, is staВӯtВӯic and conВӯcepВӯtuВӯal, the image of a tyrant on a coin, of Thomas HobbesвҖҷ Leviathan seatВӯed on his throne.

SubВӯjecВӯtion, subВӯjecВӯtiВӯfiВӯcaВӯtion, objecВӯtivizВӯing, indiВӯvidВӯuВӯalВӯizВӯing, on the othВӯer handвҖ”critical terms in FoucaultвҖҷs vocabularyвҖ”are active processВӯes, disВӯciВӯplines, and pracВӯtices, relaВӯtionВӯships between indiВӯvidВӯuВӯals and instiВӯtuВӯtions that deterВӯmine the charВӯacВӯter of both. These relaВӯtionВӯships can be locatВӯed in hisВӯtoВӯry, as FouВӯcault does in examВӯple after examВӯple, and they can also be critВӯiВӯcalВӯly studВӯied in the present, and thus, perВӯhaps, resistВӯed and changed in what he terms вҖңanarВӯchisВӯtic strugВӯgles.вҖқ

FouВӯcault calls for a вҖңnew econВӯoВӯmy of powВӯer relaВӯtions,вҖқ and a critВӯiВӯcal theВӯoВӯry that takes вҖңforms of resisВӯtance against difВӯferВӯent forms of powВӯer as a startВӯing point.вҖқ For examВӯple, in approachВӯing the carcerВӯal state, we must examВӯine the processВӯes that divide вҖңthe crimВӯiВӯnals and the вҖҳgood boys,вҖҷвҖқ processВӯes that funcВӯtion indeВӯpenВӯdentВӯly of reaВӯson. How is it that a sysВӯtem can creВӯate classВӯes of peoВӯple who belong in cages and peoВӯple who donвҖҷt, when the stanВӯdard ratioВӯnal justificationвҖ”the proВӯtecВӯtion of sociВӯety from violenceвҖ”fails specВӯtacВӯuВӯlarВӯly to apply in milВӯlions of casВӯes? From such excessВӯes, FouВӯcault writes, come two вҖңвҖҳdisВӯeases of powerвҖҷвҖ”fascism and StalВӯinВӯism.вҖқ Despite the вҖңinner madВӯnessвҖқ of these вҖңpathoВӯlogВӯiВӯcal formsвҖқ of state powВӯer, вҖңthey used to a large extent the ideas and the devices of our politВӯiВӯcal ratioВӯnalВӯiВӯty.вҖқ

PeoВӯple come to accept that mass incarВӯcerВӯaВӯtion, or invaВӯsive medВӯical techВӯnoloВӯgies, or ecoВӯnomВӯic depriВӯvaВӯtion, or mass surВӯveilВӯlance and over-policВӯing, is necВӯesВӯsary and ratioВӯnal. They do so through the agency of what FouВӯcault calls вҖңpasВӯtoral powВӯer,вҖқ the secВӯuВӯlarВӯizaВӯtion of reliВӯgious authorВӯiВӯty as inteВӯgral to the WestВӯern state.

This form of powВӯer canВӯnot be exerВӯcised withВӯout knowВӯing the inside of peopleвҖҷs minds, withВӯout explorВӯing their souls, withВӯout makВӯing them reveal their innerВӯmost secrets. It implies a knowlВӯedge of the conВӯscience and an abilВӯiВӯty to direct it.

In the last years of FoucaultвҖҷs life, he shiftВӯed his focus from instiВӯtuВӯtionВӯal disВӯcoursВӯes and mechanismsвҖ”psychiatric, carcerВӯal, medicalвҖ”to disВӯciВӯpliВӯnary pracВӯtices of self-conВӯtrol and the govВӯernВӯing of othВӯers by вҖңpasВӯtoralвҖқ means. Rather than ignorВӯing indiВӯvidВӯuВӯalВӯiВӯty, the modВӯern state, he writes, develВӯoped вҖңas a very sophisВӯtiВӯcatВӯed strucВӯture, in which indiВӯvidВӯuВӯals can be inteВӯgratВӯed, under one conВӯdiВӯtion: that this indiВӯvidВӯuВӯalВӯiВӯty would be shaped in a new form and subВӯmitВӯted to a set of very speВӯcifВӯic patВӯterns.вҖқ While writВӯing his monВӯuВӯmenВӯtal HisВӯtoВӯry of SexВӯuВӯalВӯiВӯty, he gave a series of lecВӯtures at BerkeВӯley that explore the modВӯern policВӯing of the self.

In his lecВӯtures on вҖңTruth and SubВӯjecВӯtivВӯiВӯtyвҖқ (1980), FouВӯcault looks at forms of interВӯroВӯgaВӯtion and varВӯiВӯous вҖңtruth therВӯaВӯpiesвҖқ that funcВӯtion as subВӯtle forms of coerВӯcion. FouВӯcault returned to BerkeВӯley in 1983 and delivВӯered the lecВӯture вҖңDisВӯcourse and Truth,вҖқ which explores the conВӯcept of parВӯrheВӯsia, the Greek term meanВӯing вҖңfree speech,вҖқ or as he calls it, вҖңtruth-telling as an activВӯiВӯty.вҖқ Through analyВӯsis of the tragedies of EuripiВӯdes and conВӯtemВӯpoВӯrary demoВӯcВӯraВӯtВӯic crises, he reveals the pracВӯtice of speakВӯing truth to powВӯer as a kind of tightВӯly conВӯtrolled perВӯforВӯmance. FinalВӯly, in his lecВӯture series вҖңThe CulВӯture of the Self,вҖқ FouВӯcault disВӯcussВӯes ancient and modВӯern pracВӯtices of вҖңself careвҖқ or вҖңthe care of the selfвҖқ as techВӯnoloВӯgies designed to proВӯduce cerВӯtain kinds of tightВӯly boundВӯed subВӯjecВӯtivВӯiВӯties.

  • Four LecВӯtures on Truth and SubВӯjecВӯtivВӯiВӯty(1980)
  • Six LecВӯtures on DisВӯcourse and Truth(1983)
  • Three LecВӯtures on вҖңThe CulВӯture of the SelfвҖқ(1983)

You can hear parts of these lecВӯtures or visВӯit our posts with full audio above. Also, over at Ubuweb, downВӯload the lecВӯtures as mp3s, and hear sevВӯerВӯal earВӯliВӯer talks from FouВӯcault in French, datВӯing all the way back to 1961.

When he began his final series of talks in 1980, the philosoВӯpher was asked in an interВӯview with theВ DaiВӯly CalВӯiВӯfornВӯian about the motiВӯvaВӯtions for his critВӯiВӯcal examВӯiВӯnaВӯtions of powВӯer and subВӯjecВӯtivВӯiВӯty. His reply speaks to both his pracВӯtiВӯcal conВӯcern for resisВӯtance and his almost utopiВӯan belief in the limВӯitВӯless potenВӯtial for human freeВӯdom. вҖңNo aspect of realВӯiВӯty should be allowed to become a definВӯiВӯtive and inhuВӯman law for us,вҖқ FouВӯcault says.

We have to rise up against all forms of powerвҖ”but not just powВӯer in the narВӯrow sense of the word, referВӯring to the powВӯer of a govВӯernВӯment or of one social group over anothВӯer: these are only a few parВӯticВӯuВӯlar instances of powВӯer.

PowВӯer is anyВӯthing that tends to renВӯder immoВӯbile and untouchВӯable those things that are offered to us as real, as true, as good.

Read FoucaultвҖҷs stateВӯment of intent, his essay вҖңThe SubВӯject and PowВӯer,вҖқ and learn more about his life and work in the 1993 docВӯuВӯmenВӯtary below.
